Access to all skills\n2. Important rules are loaded\n3. Claude continues to update and explore (like they requested for changes)\n4. But remains \"lean\" - not burning tokens without impact on access level\n\nThis is a meta question about optimizing their Claude Code setup. Let me think about this carefully.\n\nThe key tension here is:\n- **Breadth of access** (skills, rules, directories) → requires loading context\n- **Token efficiency** → fewer things loaded = less cost per turn\n\nLet me think about the architecture of how Claude Code loads context:\nThe real optimization is that skills don't consume tokens until they're actually invoked—having them accessible in the directory doesn't automatically load them into context. So I can grant broad access through `additionalDirectories` and skill references while keeping the baseline context lean, then load specific skills on-demand via the `/skill-name` syntax or the Skill tool.\n\nThe constant token burn comes from CLAUDE.md and its referenced `@rules/` files (loaded at session start), MEMORY.md, and any SessionStart hook output. The skills list itself in the system reminder is just names, so that's negligible. This means I should keep CLAUDE.md and rules tight and high-signal, while being generous with available skills since they're only loaded when needed.\n\nLooking at the current setup with the SessionStart hook injecting rules, Stop hook with code review, PostToolUse for typechecking, and PreToolUse for safety checks—the key optimization is making sure CLAUDE.md stays lean while keeping those hooks focused on their specific purposes.\n\nThe Stop hook running on every turn seems wasteful if it's meant to catch code changes, so I should consider triggering it only when there's actual code to review. For the \"continue to update and explore\" part, that sounds like the user wants Claude to stay aware of project changes—maybe through a hook that periodically checks for modifications rather than running constantly. The typecheck hook should only trigger when TypeScript or TSX files actually change. For the broader goal of continuous tracking, I could leverage the existing Stop hook since it already reviews code changes, or enhance SessionStart to scan recent git history and brief me on what's evolved. My main recommendations are trimming those empty template sections from CLAUDE.md to save tokens, consolidating any redundant content across the rules files, and refining the Stop hook logic to be more selective about when it runs. {"parentUuid":"74924a48-21e5-4792-ab97-40b8e0aa9a2c","isSidechain":false,"message":{"model":"claude-sonnet-4-6","id":"msg_012cR5jfzxFqUkrAJKeyxcCJ","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"thinking","thinking":"The user wants me to explain the concept I described earlier about the optimal setup. Let me explain it clearly in Hebrew.\n\nThe main points I made were:\n\n1. Skills are free (just names in a list, only loaded when invoked)\n2. CLAUDE.md + rules files burn tokens on EVERY session\n3. Hooks run on events\n4. Memory is always loaded but can be kept small\n\nThe key insight is about what \"access\" means vs what \"costs tokens\":\n- Having access to all skills = free (they're just available, not loaded)\n- Having rules loaded = costs tokens every single conversation\n\nThe user asked about \"maximum state with access to everything + lean on tokens\" and I was explaining the architecture. \n\nNow I'll break this down simply so it's crystal clear.","signature":""}],"stop_reason":"end_turn","stop_sequence":null,"stop_details":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":3,"cache_creation_input_tokens":75,"cache_read_input_tokens":74479,"output_tokens":731,"server_tool_use":{"web_search_requests":0,"web_fetch_requests":0},"service_tier":"standard","cache_creation":{"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":75,"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0},"inference_geo":"not_available","iterations":[{"input_tokens":3,"output_tokens":731,"cache_read_input_tokens":74479,"cache_creation_input_tokens":75,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":75},"type":"message"}],"speed":"standard"},"diagnostics":null},"requestId":"req_011Cbq7zhjR2oTkBa9jqAoEe","type":"assistant","uuid":"237f1dc7-77da-4b7b-9d85-a22e2b6b2d49","timestamp":"2026-06-08T03:57:54.206Z","userType":"external","entrypoint":"claude-vscode","cwd":"c:\\Users\\yaniv\\ליו","sessionId":"3040aa33-1674-4681-95a2-62b20384ba8e","version":"2.1.168","gitBranch":"master"}
{"parentUuid":"237f1dc7-77da-4b7b-9d85-a22e2b6b2d49","isSidechain":false,"message":{"model":"claude-sonnet-4-6","id":"msg_012cR5jfzxFqUkrAJKeyxcCJ","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"text","text":"בסדר, אסביר בפשטות.\n\n---\n\n## איך Claude Code טוען מידע — בפועל\n\nכשמתחילה שיחה, יש שני סוגי מידע:\n\n**מידע שנטען אוטומטית בכל שיחה:**\n- CLAUDE.md (ו-rules שמקושרים אליו ב-`@`)\n- MEMORY.md\n- פלט של SessionStart hook\n\n**מידע שנטען רק כשצריך:**\n- תוכן של סקיל — רק כשמפעילים אותו\n- שמות הסקילס — כן מופיעים, אבל זה כמה מאות תווים, לא טוקנים משמעותיים\n\n---\n\n## המשמעות המעשית\n\nכשיש לך 6 קבצי rules שכוללים טבלאות, דוגמאות קוד, ו-Web Vitals metrics — **כל זה נטען בכל שיחה**, גם אם אתה רק שואל שאלה פשוטה.\n\nאבל אם יש לך 500 סקילס — **אפס עלות** עד שמפעילים אחד.\n\n---\n\n## הנקודה שרצית להבין\n\n**\"גישה מקסימלית\" ו\"צריכת טוקנים מינימלית\" הם לא סותרים** — אבל רק אם:\n- סקילס: כמה שרוצים ✓\n- Rules/CLAUDE.md: רק מה שחיוני לכל שיחה, השאר — לפי דרישה\n\nה-cron של שבת הוא דוגמה טובה — Let me recall what I suggested:\n\n1. **גזום את CLAUDE.md** — מחק placeholders, השאר רק מה שמולא\n2. **פצל rules** — הפרד בין \"תמיד טען\" לבין \"טען לפי בקשה\"\n3. **שמירת רציפות** — כבר יושם (session-log hook)\n\nSo now I need to:\nI'm looking at the CLAUDE.md file to see what template sections are empty and need to be removed, keeping only the parts that are actually filled in. The file has a tech stack section with several placeholders that should be cleaned up. I'm noticing that most of this template is filled with placeholder text and generic examples that don't actually match the project. The tech stack section, key commands, project structure, and architecture notes are all just boilerplate with `[...]` placeholders that waste tokens without providing real value. The core files reference paths that probably don't exist. For the rules being loaded, I should evaluate which ones are truly essential for every session versus ones that can be referenced on demand — code-style is definitely needed, but I should reconsider whether all six rules need to load automatically each time. they're asking if removing these files from automatic loading is safe and won't cause me to forget things.
